In a climate-controlled garage workshop lined with vintage oscilloscopes and spare vacuum tubes, the persona disassembles a non-functional Friden EC-130 calculator. Using a magnifying lamp and tweezers made from 1950s-era surgical instruments, they clean the mechanical relay contacts with isopropyl alcohol and a camel-hair brush. They inspect the cathode-ray tube display’s phosphor coating for burn-in patterns and document the sequence of vacuum tube failures (including the 6SN7GT and 6AU6A) using a handwritten logbook with carbon paper copies—mimicking 1960s lab practices. They replace one faulty 12AT7 tube with a NOS (New Old Stock) unit procured from a collector in Germany.

1:00 PM – Lunch with a Side of Vacuum Fluorescent Display Theory  
Over a packed lunch of peanut butter and banana on rye (a 1960s-era “space age” sandwich), the persona listens to an oral history interview with a former General Electric engineer who worked on early VFD (Vacuum Fluorescent Display) prototypes. They pause the audio to sketch the electrode grid geometry of a 1964 GE VFD on a napkin, comparing it to the display architecture of the 1967 Sharp Compet CS-10A. They reflect on why VFDs eventually replaced Nixie tubes: lower voltage requirements, faster response, and failsafe design—where a broken filament doesn’t cause a total display failure, unlike Nixie tubes’ single-cathode dependency.
